citation: Wusk, Z., & Rowson, S. (2022). Football Shoulder Pad Design and Its Effect on Head Kinematics in Shoulder-to-Helmet Impacts. Annals of Biomedical Engineering. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10439-022-03063-3
abstract: Shoulder-to-helmet (STH) impacts account for approximately 20% of concussions in football, yet no published research had examined football shoulder pad design in relation to STH impacts. This study compared an unmodified Douglas SW24 flat pad (control) to a modified version with added Vinyl Nitrile 600 closed-cell foam at the top flap, interior arch, and shoulder resting point. Twenty participants with varsity-level football experience each performed six impacts against a helmeted Hybrid III dummy equipped with a NOCSAE instrumented head, yielding 111 analyzable trials. The modified shoulder pad produced statistically significant reductions in peak linear acceleration (31%), peak rotational acceleration (28%), and peak rotational velocity (10%), while increasing impact duration by 40%. Impact response corridors were developed for both conditions to support future controlled lab replication of STH impacts.
tldr: Adding foam padding to football shoulder pads meaningfully reduces the head kinematics generated during shoulder-to-helmet impacts. A 31% drop in peak linear acceleration and a 28% drop in rotational acceleration were observed at realistic impact speeds, suggesting that shoulder pad design—an area currently without performance standards—is a viable lever for reducing concussion risk in football. Impact response corridors developed in this study can guide future higher-speed testing to evaluate padding performance at concussive impact levels.
methods: Prospective lab study; 20 male participants with varsity football experience each delivered 6 impacts (3 per pad condition) to the upper-side of a helmeted Hybrid III 50th-percentile dummy suspended at 40 degrees. Participants used a 5-m runway before striking. Head kinematics captured at 20,000 Hz via three linear accelerometers and three angular rate sensors (NOCSAE head). High-speed cameras (960 fps) measured impact speed and contact location. Nine impacts removed due to off-target contact. Paired one-sided t-tests (alpha = 0.05) compared conditions; impact response corridors built using a modified Maltese method.
findings: Modified (foam-added) shoulder pad vs. control: peak linear acceleration reduced 31% (29.4 g to 20.3 g, p<0.001); peak rotational acceleration reduced 28% (1960 to 1396 rad/s², p<0.001); peak rotational velocity reduced 10% (22.8 to 20.4 rad/s, p<0.001); impact duration increased 40% (24.8 to 34.8 ms, p<0.001). Average impact speed was consistent across conditions (5.8 m/s). Control pad rotational velocities approached published concussive thresholds (~22 rad/s).
limitations: Impact speeds (mean 5.8 m/s) were below concussive levels reported in NFL studies (~9.3 m/s), limiting generalizability to high-severity impacts. Hybrid III dummy biofidelity for lateral STH impacts is uncertain. Variability in participant striking technique, though mitigated by paired analysis design. Ordered/learning effects not fully controlled. No existing shoulder pad performance standards for comparison.
practicalImplications: Shoulder pad design is an underexplored, actionable target for concussion risk reduction in football. Adding closed-cell foam at the top flap, arch, and resting point of existing pads is a low-cost modification that meaningfully reduces head kinematics in shoulder-to-helmet impacts. Programs should advocate for shoulder pad performance standards similar to those for helmets. Future higher-speed testing is needed before definitive concussion-risk claims can be made.
athleteDev: For developing players at all levels, shoulder pad design may contribute to long-term head safety. Programs should consider padding quality in shoulder pad selection criteria, not just fit or position-specific coverage. Coaches and equipment managers should be aware that STH impacts—now comprising ~22% of NFL concussions—are an increasing share of head contact events as helmet-to-helmet rules tighten.
rtp: Not directly applicable to RTP decision-making. However, the study underscores that modifiable equipment factors (shoulder pad design) contribute to concussion exposure, which has downstream implications for cumulative head impact burden and RTP thresholds. Players with concussion history may benefit from upgraded shoulder pad padding as a secondary prevention measure.

citation: Eichner, E. R. (2018). Football Team Rhabdomyolysis: The Pain Beats the Gain and the Coach Is to Blame. Current Sports Medicine Reports, 17(5), 142–143.
abstract: This clinical commentary reviews 17 outbreaks of team exertional rhabdomyolysis (ER) across multiple sports over recent years, with particular focus on seven football outbreaks at college and high school levels. Documented outbreaks occurred at Iowa (2011, 13 hospitalized), Maine (2012), Oregon (2017), Nebraska (2018), and multiple high school programs. The paper identifies a consistent causal pattern: novel, high-volume exercise performed immediately after a period of deconditioning (typically winter or summer break), directed by coaches motivated to 'toughen up' players following a losing season. The author argues that in each case, institutional and coaching decisions—not individual player failure—were the proximate cause of ER outbreaks.
tldr: Team exertional rhabdomyolysis (ER) in football is a preventable, coach-driven phenomenon. Every documented outbreak shares the same profile: novel overexertion (too much, too soon, too fast) after a deconditioning break, often as punishment or to assert a new coaching philosophy. Strength and conditioning staff must resist pressure to implement extreme novel loading in the first sessions after any break, as the combination of deconditioning and unfamiliar high-volume exercise reliably precipitates ER requiring hospitalization.
methods: Narrative clinical review describing 17 team ER outbreaks across college and high school sports. The author synthesizes published case reports, conference presentations, and media-documented incidents. Cases span football (7), swimming (2), lacrosse (2), soccer (1), track (1), basketball (1), softball (1), volleyball (1), and golf (1). Epidemiological patterns across outbreaks are described qualitatively.
findings: All 17 outbreaks shared the same etiology: novel overexertion following a break from training. Football outbreaks predominantly involved squat-based or weightlifting protocols administered in the first 1-3 days after winter break. Multiple outbreaks required fasciotomies for compartment syndrome. Coaches consistently blamed athletes rather than accepting institutional responsibility. Programs with new coaches or following losing seasons were disproportionately represented. The Iowa outbreak (100 timed back squats at 50% 1RM on day 2 of return) produced 13 hospitalizations—the largest single football ER event documented.
limitations: Narrative review without systematic search methodology. Reliance on published case reports and media accounts introduces selection bias toward more severe or publicized outbreaks. Causality is inferred from temporal and contextual patterns rather than prospective study design. Individual player-level physiological risk factors are not analyzed.
practicalImplications: The first training session after any break (winter, summer, postseason) should never introduce novel, high-volume exercises at near-maximal intensity. Strength and conditioning staff must follow evidence-based return-to-training progressions regardless of coaching pressure. Institutions should have explicit rhabdomyolysis prevention protocols including graduated loading after deconditioning periods. Early recognition of ER (severe muscle pain and weakness 12-48 hours post-exercise, dark urine) and immediate medical referral is essential. Culture change is needed so coaches view ER outbreaks as institutional failures, not player weakness.
athleteDev: Young football athletes are particularly vulnerable to ER because they may push through pain to avoid social stigma or dismissal from the team. Strength and conditioning programs must implement standardized return-to-training ramp protocols after all breaks exceeding one week. Novel movements (e.g., back squats, plyometrics) should be introduced at low volumes before increasing load or repetitions, regardless of prior training history.
rtp: Athletes who suffer ER require medical clearance before returning to full training. Severe cases involving compartment syndrome and fasciotomy have prolonged RTP timelines. Recurrence risk is elevated in players with prior ER history, and graduated loading should be especially conservative on subsequent return-to-play after breaks. Institutions should document all ER cases to identify repeat patterns and modify programming accordingly.

citation: Hecksteden, A., Schmartz, G. P., Egyptien, Y., aus der Fünten, K., Keller, A., & Meyer, T. (2022). Forecasting football injuries by combining screening, monitoring and machine learning. Science and Medicine in Football. https://doi.org/10.1080/24733938.2022.2095006
abstract: Injury risk management in football requires identifying players and circumstances associated with elevated injury likelihood. Prior work has typically applied either screening (time-stable characteristics) or monitoring (volatile daily factors) data separately, limiting predictive scope. This prospective observational cohort study developed and validated a machine learning injury forecasting model integrating both data types in 88 male professional football (soccer) players from 4 German 3rd and 4th division teams during the 2019/2020 season. Gradient boosting with ROSE upsampling within player-level leave-one-out cross-validation was used to predict acute, non-contact, time-loss injuries on a daily basis. The integrated model achieved a cross-validated ROC-AUC of 0.61 and a holdout test set AUC of 0.62, outperforming comparator models using monitoring data alone.
tldr: Combining periodic fitness screening data with daily monitoring data in a gradient-boosted machine learning model produces better injury forecasting accuracy than monitoring alone in professional football. The model's ROC-AUC of ~0.62 on a holdout test set indicates fair but not clinically sufficient discrimination. Key predictors included pain during the Football Injury Movement Screen (SIMS_pain), match day exposure, and post-RTP vulnerability windows. Prospective external validation is required before practical deployment, but the integration of screening and monitoring data represents a methodologically sound step toward actionable injury risk tools.
methods: Prospective observational cohort study; 88 male professional football players from 4 teams (German 3rd/4th division), 2019/2020 season (June 2019 to March 2020, truncated at COVID-19 shutdown). Data collected: basic player info, periodic screening (endurance capacity, 30m sprint, body composition, SIMS movement screen), daily monitoring (session RPE training load, subjective recovery/stress scale), exposure type (match vs. training), and injury recording per Fuller consensus. Gradient boosting (R) with ROSE upsampling integrated into player-level LOOCV. 11 explanatory variables preselected a priori. Holdout test set = 25% of players, stratified by injury history. Ancillary analyses probed leaky folds, upsampling effects, and round-robin robustness.
findings: 51 criterion injuries (acute, non-contact, time-loss) in 88 players over the study period. Main model cross-validated ROC-AUC = 0.61 [0.60–0.62]; holdout test set AUC = 0.62 [0.49–0.76]. Comparator models (monitoring only: AUC 0.56 on test set; monitoring + basic info: 0.52 on test set) were inferior, supporting value of integrating screening data. Top predictors: SIMS_pain, match day, post-RTP period, striker position, team-average sessionRPE. Leaky-folds analysis confirmed that ignoring hierarchical data structure inflates cross-validation AUC to 0.88—highlighting a major methodological risk in prior published injury ML models. Approximately 50% of criterion injuries concentrated in the 12.5% of time-points with highest predicted risk.
limitations: Small absolute number of criterion injuries (n=51) limits model stability; substantial variability in round-robin repetitions confirms lack of robustness. Single season data from one country's lower professional divisions limits generalizability. COVID-19 truncated the study period. Model miscalibration (overestimation of injury risk) due to upsampling. No prospective temporal validation attempted. Feature engineering and modeling choices must be adapted for each club's specific data collection infrastructure.
practicalImplications: Integrating periodic fitness screening with daily monitoring data via machine learning offers a methodologically sound framework for injury risk stratification in professional football. Programs already collecting both data types can leverage this approach without additional testing burden. However, current accuracy (~0.62 AUC) is insufficient for high-stakes clinical decisions (e.g., mandatory rest); the model's value lies in flagging elevated-risk periods to prompt clinical conversations, not in replacing human judgment. Prioritizing vigilance during match days, post-RTP windows, and in players with movement screen pain findings is supported by the feature importance results.
athleteDev: The framework is relevant for development programs collecting screening and wellness data. Movement screen pain (SIMS_pain) and match day exposure were among the top injury risk signals, suggesting that even in younger developmental players, systematic integration of physical screening with daily load data could improve injury surveillance. Programs should invest in standardized data collection infrastructure to enable future ML-based risk tools as sample sizes accumulate.
rtp: The post-RTP vulnerability window was identified as one of the most important predictors of subsequent injury in this model. Specifically, a 4-week period following return to full availability carried elevated risk. This empirically supports conservative monitoring protocols in the month after RTP, with heightened attention to training load, subjective recovery, and movement quality during this window.

citation: Nguyen, Q., Jiang, R., Ellingwood, M., & Yurko, R. (2025). Fractional tackles: leveraging player tracking data for within-play tackling evaluation in American football. Scientific Reports, 15, 2148. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-85993-1
abstract: Traditional tackling statistics in American football (solo tackles, assists, combined tackles) are discrete, manually recorded outcomes that undercount defensive contributions—particularly for linemen—and overstate the contributions credited to a single tackler. Using NFL Next Gen Stats player tracking data from the 2024 Big Data Bowl (weeks 1-9 of the 2022 NFL season, 5,539 run plays by running backs), this paper introduces fractional tackles: a continuous, model-free metric that credits defenders for slowing the ball-carrier's forward motion toward the end zone during contact windows within a play. The metric identifies contact windows (consecutive frames where a defender is within 1.5 yards of the ball-carrier), assigns a value to each window based on velocity reduction, and distributes credit across all involved defenders proportionally by frame and player count.
tldr: Fractional tackles is a new, data-driven defensive metric derived from NFL player tracking data that overcomes the binary limitations of traditional tackle counts. By measuring how much each defender contributes to slowing the ball-carrier's forward velocity during contact, the metric credits defenders who are not officially recorded as tacklers—especially defensive linemen who consistently disrupt plays but rarely receive statistical recognition. The metric shows strong correlation with traditional tackles (r=0.93) but greater stability across time and more measurable information for players without conventional box-score statistics.
methods: NFL Big Data Bowl 2024 tracking data used (10 Hz, 22 players + ball tracked per play); 5,539 run plays by running backs from weeks 1-9 of the 2022 NFL regular season. Contact windows defined as consecutive frames where a defender is within 1.5 yards of the ball-carrier (threshold covering ~95% of tackle distances in the data). Contact window value = (starting velocity - ending velocity) / peak pre-contact velocity, with adjustments for post-contact velocity recovery. Window value distributed equally across frames; frame value distributed equally across defenders in contact. Fractional tackles summed per defender per window and aggregated across plays. Stability assessed by correlating first-4-week vs. last-5-week totals. Compared to traditional combined tackles (total tackles + assists/2).
findings: 7,453 contact windows identified across 5,539 plays; average window length 1.28 seconds. Top fractional tackles leaders were dominated by linebackers (ILBs, OLBs) but defensive linemen showed notably higher average fractional tackles per play than their traditional tackle counts suggest. Fractional tackles correlated strongly with traditional combined tackles (r=0.93, 95% CI: 0.92–0.94) but were systematically lower, indicating conventional statistics overstate single-defender credit. Fractional tackles demonstrated greater stability (predictability of future from past performance) than combined tackles: overall r=0.69 vs. 0.59 across the season, with especially stronger within-position stability for linebackers (0.73 vs. 0.64). The metric credits 19,691 unique players across the study period, more than double the 7,720 with at least one recorded tackle.
limitations: Analysis limited to run plays by running backs (weeks 1-9 of one season), limiting generalizability to pass plays, other ball-carrier types, and full seasons. Contact windows are empirically defined from 2D tracking data; true physical contact cannot be confirmed without pose estimation. Credit attribution assumes equal distribution across defenders per frame, which simplifies actual contribution weighting. Ball-carrier velocity recovery between windows not fully credited to subsequent defenders. Framework is player-agnostic in contact window definition and does not account for player-specific traits (e.g., arm length) that affect contact radius.
practicalImplications: Fractional tackles provides coaching and analytics staffs with a more granular, objective view of defensive contribution than traditional tackle counts. Defensive linemen—a position group historically underserved by box-score statistics—can now be evaluated on measurable stopping contribution per play. The metric can inform game planning (identifying defenders most effective at halting specific ball-carriers), contract negotiations, and draft evaluation. Because it is model-free and derived from publicly available tracking data, it can be implemented in any data pipeline with access to player positioning.
athleteDev: For developing defensive players, fractional tackles offers a more informative feedback loop than binary tackle/no-tackle designations. Linemen and interior defenders who routinely reduce ball-carrier momentum but are not credited with official tackles can have their contributions quantified, supporting more accurate development assessments and reducing positional evaluation bias toward high-tackle-count positions like linebackers and safeties.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play contexts. However, the framework's ability to quantify within-play physical engagement could theoretically be adapted to monitor a returning player's on-field contribution and physical activity levels during gradual reintegration, providing objective data on participation intensity beyond simple snap count or playing time metrics.

citation: Palermi, S., Vittadini, F., Pedret, C., Vecchiato, M., Della Villa, F., Corsini, A., Jokela, A., & Lempainen, L. (2025). From the Field to the Screen: A Scoping Review of Video Analysis as a Tool for Understanding Thigh Muscle-Tendon Injuries in Football. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine. https://doi.org/10.1097/JSM.0000000000001377
abstract: Thigh muscle-tendon injuries are among the most prevalent and costly injuries in professional football, with hamstrings, adductors, quadriceps (rectus femoris), and calf muscles collectively affected. This scoping review synthesized data from 10 studies published 2010-2025 that used video analysis to investigate thigh muscle-tendon injury mechanisms in football players. Studies were quality-appraised using the QA-SIVAS scale. The review identified noncontact and indirect contact as the predominant mechanisms for severe injuries. Common injury scenarios included sprinting-induced eccentric hamstring strains, kicking-related rectus femoris injuries, and closed kinetic chain adductor longus injuries. Video analysis is positioned as an essential tool for understanding injury biomechanics, informing prevention, and guiding rehabilitation—with AI-enhanced video analysis identified as a critical future direction.
tldr: Video analysis of professional football injuries consistently shows that severe thigh muscle-tendon injuries are primarily noncontact or indirect-contact events driven by eccentric loading during high-speed running, kicking, and sudden directional changes. Hamstring injuries predominantly occur during sprinting (acceleration/deceleration), rectus femoris injuries during kicking and sprinting, and adductor injuries during complex closed and open kinetic chain movements. Prevention strategies should target eccentric strength in these positions and movement patterns, not simply limit high-speed running exposure—as many injuries occur during short accelerations and unexpected situations, not necessarily at maximal sprint speed.
methods: Scoping review methodology; comprehensive search of PubMed, Scopus, Cochrane, Embase, SportDiscuss, and Google Scholar from inception to February 2025, supplemented by AI-assisted preliminary mapping via ResearchRabbit. Inclusion criteria: video analysis as a primary method, thigh muscle-tendon injuries in professional football, acute injuries only. Ten studies met inclusion criteria. Quality assessed with the 18-item QA-SIVAS scale. Data extracted: objectives, number of injuries analyzed, muscle groups, and main injury mechanism findings.
findings: All 10 included studies found that severe thigh muscle-tendon injuries are predominantly noncontact (54-83% across studies). Hamstring: sprint-related (acceleration/deceleration) and stretch-related (CKC) mechanisms; biceps femoris long head most affected; injuries occur at high horizontal speeds but also during short accelerations <10m (86% involved running per Vermeulen et al.). Rectus femoris: kicking is the dominant mechanism (80% per Jokela et al.); complete ruptures common with kicking/sprinting. Adductor longus: change of direction (35%), kicking (29%), reaching (24%), jumping (12%); predominantly CKC movements with hip extension, abduction, external rotation. Gender differences noted: women have higher proportion of noncontact injuries during running and kicking than men. Eccentric muscle activation during rapid loading is consistently implicated across all injury types.
limitations: Evidence classified as Level 3 (Oxford Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine)—observational studies preclude causal inference. Methodological variability across studies (QA-SIVAS scores varied; no control groups in any study). Findings specific to professional football; limited applicability to amateur or youth levels. Only 10 studies included, restricting breadth of conclusions. Reliance on peer-reviewed journals may introduce publication bias. Practical implementation of video-based injury prevention in real-world settings faces cost and infrastructure barriers.
practicalImplications: Prevention programs should emphasize eccentric strengthening of hamstrings (Nordic curls, Romanian deadlifts), hip adductors, and quadriceps rather than solely limiting high-speed running volumes—as injuries commonly occur at submaximal speeds and during unexpected situational demands. Kicking mechanics and load management during kicking-intensive training phases are particularly relevant for rectus femoris injury prevention. Video analysis of training and match footage should be incorporated into injury surveillance programs to identify individual movement patterns associated with elevated injury risk. Return-to-play protocols should assess movement quality in the specific kinematic patterns (sprinting, kicking, CKC reaching) associated with each injury type.
athleteDev: Developing football athletes should progressively build eccentric strength capacity in hamstrings, adductors, and quadriceps from early in their career to withstand the high eccentric demands of sprinting and kicking. Position-specific loading patterns should inform training design: wide receivers and midfielders face greater hamstring sprint demands; forwards and attacking midfielders face higher rectus femoris kicking loads. Video-based movement screening can identify athletes whose running and kicking mechanics place excessive eccentric demand on specific muscle groups.
rtp: Video analysis findings directly inform RTP criteria for thigh muscle-tendon injuries. Functional RTP assessments should include sport-specific movement tests corresponding to the injury mechanism: maximal sprint at progressive speeds for hamstring injuries, kicking mechanics assessment for rectus femoris injuries, and CKC loading tests (change of direction, lunging) for adductor injuries. The review supports monitoring movement quality—not just pain absence or strength ratios—as a criterion for progressive RTP in professional football.
